title: Introduction to finance
summary: Finance is the lifeblood of every business — no organisation starts, survives or grows without it. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 3.1: the role and importance of finance in a business; the difference between capital expenditure (spending on fixed, non-current assets) and revenue expenditure (day-to-day running costs); why businesses need finance in the first place (start-up, working capital and growth or expansion); and an overview of internal versus external sources, whose detail belongs to 3.2. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see why an applied, named-business answer, not a generic definition, is what earns the marks.
learningObjectives: Explain the role and importance of finance in a business.
Distinguish between capital expenditure (spending on fixed/non-current assets) and revenue expenditure (day-to-day running costs), giving examples of each.
Explain why businesses need finance — for start-up, for working capital and for growth or expansion.
Outline the difference between internal and external sources of finance (with detailed comparison reserved for 3.2).
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Money Matters: Fuelling Your Business
simpleExplanation.summary: Finance is the money a business needs to start, to keep running day to day, and to grow. Understanding where that money is spent — and why it is needed — is the foundation of every financial decision that follows.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think about owning a car. Buying the car itself is a huge, one-off payment that lasts for years, like a business buying a new factory — that is capital expenditure. The regular costs of petrol, insurance and minor repairs are like the day-to-day running costs of a business, such as wages or electricity bills — that is revenue expenditure. You might dip into long-term savings for the car, but you cover the running costs out of your monthly salary. A business faces exactly the same split, and choosing the right kind of money for each kind of spending is the whole game.
steps: First, identify WHY the business needs money — to start up, to cover day-to-day running (working capital), or to grow and expand.
Next, categorise the spending — is it a major, long-lasting fixed asset (capital expenditure) or a short-term, operational cost (revenue expenditure)?
Then, take an overview of WHERE the money can come from — inside the business (internal) or from outside it (external). The detailed comparison of each source is in 3.2.
Finally, remember the golden rule: match the type of finance to the need, so that long-term assets are funded with long-term money and short-term costs with short-term finance.

flashcards.back: The money (capital) a business requires to start up, to run day to day and to grow. It is often called the 'lifeblood' of a business because without it the business cannot operate or survive.

Spending on fixed (non-current) assets — items the business keeps and uses for more than one year, such as machinery, buildings, vehicles or equipment. It is a long-term investment intended to help the business generate revenue over time.

Spending on the day-to-day running of a business, such as wages, rent, utility bills, insurance and raw materials. These are recurring operational costs incurred to earn the current period's revenue; they are used up quickly.
An item a business owns and uses for more than one accounting year to help it operate — e.g. a delivery van, an oven, a factory. Buying or upgrading one is capital expenditure.

The finance a business needs for its day-to-day operations — the money that covers short-term costs such as paying suppliers, wages and bills while waiting for customers to pay. A business can be profitable yet still fail if it runs out of working capital.
The initial finance needed to get a new business off the ground before it earns any revenue — covering premises, equipment, initial stock, legal fees and early running costs.
Funds generated from within the business itself, without borrowing or bringing in outsiders. The main examples are retained profit and the sale of existing assets. (Compared in detail in 3.2.)

Funds obtained from sources outside the business, such as bank loans, overdrafts, share capital, trade credit or grants. It gives access to larger sums but usually carries a cost (interest) or a loss of control. (Detailed in 3.2.)

The test is the LIFESPAN of what is bought, not the amount spent. A £30,000 fixed asset (van) is capital expenditure; £30,000 of wages or stock is revenue expenditure. Big spending is not automatically 'capital'.

Repairing a broken window is revenue expenditure (restoring a day-to-day cost). Replacing all windows with better double glazing that improves the asset is capital expenditure. The distinction is maintenance versus improvement of a fixed asset.

A profitable business can still collapse if it lacks working capital to pay wages and suppliers on time. Needing finance for day-to-day operations is not a sign of being unprofitable — it is normal and constant.

faq.q: What is the difference between capital expenditure and revenue expenditure?
faq.a: Capital expenditure is money spent acquiring or upgrading fixed (non-current) assets — items the business keeps and uses for more than a year, like machinery, vehicles or buildings. Revenue expenditure is money spent on the day-to-day running of the business — wages, rent, utilities, insurance and raw materials — which is used up in the current period. The deciding test is how long the benefit lasts, not how large the sum is: a small tool that lasts years can be capital expenditure, while a huge wage bill is revenue expenditure.

faq.q: Why does a business need finance if it is already making sales?
faq.a: Because sales revenue and cash rarely arrive at the same time, and because operating, replacing assets and growing all cost money up front. A business needs finance for three broad reasons: to START UP (before any revenue comes in), for WORKING CAPITAL (to cover day-to-day costs such as wages, stock and bills while it waits for customers to pay), and for GROWTH or EXPANSION (new premises, machinery or products). Even a profitable, growing business constantly needs finance to bridge these gaps.

faq.q: What is working capital and why does it matter so much?
faq.a: Working capital is the finance available for a business's everyday operations — the money that pays suppliers, wages and bills before customers' payments come in. It matters because a business can be profitable on paper yet still fail if it runs out of cash to meet its immediate obligations. Managing working capital — the timing of money in and out — is one of the most important jobs of the finance function.

faq.q: What is the difference between internal and external sources of finance?
faq.a: Internal finance comes from within the business — mainly retained profit (profit kept back rather than paid out) and the sale of unwanted assets. It is generally cheap, carries no interest and does not dilute control, but the amounts available are limited. External finance comes from outside — bank loans, overdrafts, share capital, trade credit, grants and so on. It can provide much larger sums for growth, but usually brings a cost (interest) or a loss of control (issuing shares). This lesson gives the overview; 3.2 compares each specific source in detail.

faq.q: Why can't a business just use a short-term overdraft to buy a new factory?
faq.a: This is the 'principle of matching': the term of the finance should match the lifespan of what it funds. A factory is a long-term fixed asset (capital expenditure), so it needs long-term finance such as a mortgage or share capital. An overdraft is a short-term source the bank can demand back at any time and usually charges high, variable interest on — using it to buy a long-term asset creates serious financial risk. Long-term assets need long-term money; short-term costs use short-term finance.

sections.content: Every decision a business makes — hiring an employee, launching a product, opening a branch — has a financial consequence. Finance is so central that it is often called the lifeblood of a business: cut off the flow and the organisation cannot start, cannot run and cannot grow. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 3.1 — the role and importance of finance, the crucial difference between capital and revenue expenditure, why businesses actually need finance (for start-up, working capital and growth), and an overview of where that finance can come from, internal versus external. The detailed comparison of each individual source belongs to 3.2; here we build the foundations that the rest of the Finance and accounts unit rests on. Because these ideas power every later financial topic, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why an applied answer beats a memorised definition.
The role and importance of finance

The finance function is the nerve centre of a business's financial operations, and its job goes far beyond 'counting the money'. It manages the flow of funds so the business can meet its day-to-day commitments while also planning for long-term investment and growth. Finance is important because it is needed at every stage of the business's life: to get started, to keep the doors open, and to expand. Crucially, even a profitable business can collapse if it mismanages its cash — running out of money to pay wages or suppliers on time. That is why sound financial management, not just healthy sales, decides whether a business survives.

items: **Recording transactions:** keeping accurate records of all income and expenditure so the business knows exactly where it stands.
**Preparing financial statements:** producing key documents such as the income statement and the balance sheet.
**Managing cash flow and working capital:** monitoring money moving in and out so the business can always meet its short-term obligations (its liquidity).
**Budgeting and forecasting:** planning future financial performance and anticipating funding needs before they become emergencies.
**Sourcing finance:** identifying and securing the right funds for both day-to-day operations and long-term investment.

sections.content: A foundation concept in business finance is the distinction between two types of spending. Capital expenditure is money spent acquiring or upgrading fixed (non-current) assets — long-lasting items such as machinery, vehicles, equipment or buildings that the business will use for more than one year to help it operate. Revenue expenditure is money spent on the day-to-day running of the business — wages, rent, utilities, insurance and raw materials — costs that are used up in the current period to earn that period's revenue. The deciding test is the LIFESPAN of what is bought, not the size of the payment: a large wage bill is still revenue expenditure, while a modestly priced but long-lasting tool is capital expenditure. Getting this split right matters for accurate accounting, for tax, and for matching the right kind of finance to each kind of spending.

items: **Capital expenditure** — buys or upgrades fixed (non-current) assets used for more than a year. Examples: delivery van, oven, machinery, factory, computers. It is a long-term investment; the asset appears on the balance sheet.

**Revenue expenditure** — pays for day-to-day running costs used up in the period. Examples: wages, rent, electricity, raw materials, insurance. It is a recurring operating cost; it appears on the income statement.
**The test is lifespan, not amount:** big spending is not automatically capital — £40,000 of stock is revenue expenditure, while a £400 tool that lasts years is capital.
**Repair vs upgrade:** repairing a broken machine is revenue expenditure; replacing it with a better one that improves the asset is capital expenditure.

sections.question:
A bakery, 'The Rolling Pin Ltd.', incurred the following costs in March:
- Purchase of a new delivery van: £25,000
- Flour and sugar supplies: £3,500
- Monthly rent for the shop: £2,000
- Installation of a new, larger oven: £15,000
- Wages for two bakers: £4,000

Categorise each item as either capital expenditure or revenue expenditure and calculate the total for each category.
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The test for each item is whether it buys a long-lasting fixed asset (capital) or covers a day-to-day running cost that is used up in the period (revenue).

**Capital expenditure — fixed assets lasting more than a year:**
- New delivery van (£25,000): a fixed asset The Rolling Pin will use for several years to deliver its goods.
- New, larger oven (£15,000): production equipment that will last well beyond one year.
- **Total capital expenditure = £25,000 + £15,000 = £40,000.**

**Revenue expenditure — day-to-day running costs:**
- Flour and sugar (£3,500): raw materials used up in this month's baking.
- Monthly rent (£2,000): a recurring operating cost for using the premises.
- Wages for two bakers (£4,000): the cost of labour for the current month.
- **Total revenue expenditure = £3,500 + £2,000 + £4,000 = £9,500.**

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks.** This is an AO1/AO2 categorisation task, so the engine credits each correct classification tied to the reason it qualifies:

- **AO1:** correct understanding that capital expenditure is spending on fixed assets and revenue expenditure is day-to-day running cost.
- **AO2:** each item correctly classified for THIS bakery — van and oven as capital because they are long-lasting assets; flour, rent and wages as revenue because they are consumed this month — plus the two correct totals (£40,000 and £9,500).

Notice the £25,000 van is a large sum but is still capital, while the £4,000 wages, though smaller, is revenue: the engine rewards using the lifespan test, not the price tag.

sections.content: Businesses need finance for three broad purposes, and a strong answer names the right one for the situation in front of it. First, START-UP: a new business needs finance before it earns a single pound of revenue — to buy or rent premises, purchase equipment and initial stock, pay legal and set-up fees, and cover early running costs. Second, WORKING CAPITAL: an established business constantly needs finance for day-to-day operations, paying wages, suppliers and bills while it waits for its own customers to pay. This is why a profitable business can still fail — profit on paper is not the same as cash in the bank. Third, GROWTH or EXPANSION: a business that wants to open new branches, buy more machinery, enter new markets or develop new products needs finance to fund that investment, usually well before the extra revenue arrives.

items: **Start-up:** the initial capital to launch — premises, equipment, initial stock, legal fees and early costs, all needed before any revenue is earned.
**Working capital:** finance for day-to-day operations — paying wages, suppliers and bills while waiting for customers to pay. A recurring, ongoing need, not a sign of weakness.
**Growth / expansion:** finance to fund investment in new premises, machinery, markets or products — usually required up front, ahead of the extra revenue it generates.
**Replacing assets:** an ongoing need to finance the replacement of worn-out or outdated fixed assets so the business keeps operating efficiently.

sections.content: When a question asks why a business needs finance, always match the reason to the case. A brand-new business needs START-UP capital; an established firm struggling to pay wages while it waits for customer payments needs WORKING CAPITAL; a firm opening new branches needs finance for GROWTH. Naming the specific purpose for that specific business is where the application (AO2) marks are won — 'it needs money' on its own earns almost nothing.
An overview of sources: internal versus external

Once we know WHY a business needs finance, the next question is WHERE it comes from. At this level the key split is between internal and external sources; the detailed comparison of each individual source — its cost, its risk and when it is appropriate — is the job of 3.2. Internal finance is generated from within the business itself, without borrowing or bringing in outsiders. Its two main forms are retained profit (profit kept back rather than distributed to owners) and the sale of unwanted or under-used assets. Internal finance is attractive because it is cheap, carries no interest and does not dilute the owners' control — but the amounts available are limited. External finance is injected from outside the business: bank loans, overdrafts, share capital, trade credit, grants and more. It can raise the large sums needed for growth, but it usually comes at a cost — interest to repay, or a share of ownership and control given away. Neither is automatically 'better'; the right choice depends on the amount needed, the purpose, the cost, the risk and the legal structure of the business.

items: **Internal finance — from within the business:** mainly retained profit and the sale of assets. Cheap, no interest, no loss of control — but limited in amount.

**External finance — from outside the business:** e.g. bank loans, overdrafts, share capital, trade credit, grants. Access to larger sums for growth — but usually a cost (interest) or a loss of control (issuing shares).

**Matching principle:** long-term assets (capital expenditure) should be funded by long-term finance; short-term costs (working capital) by short-term finance. Using a short-term overdraft to buy a factory is a classic error.
**Detail comes next:** the specific advantages, disadvantages and appropriateness of each source are compared fully in 3.2 — here you only need the internal/external overview.

**Judging capital vs revenue expenditure by the amount spent** — the test is the LIFESPAN of what is bought, not its price. A large stock or wage bill is revenue expenditure; a long-lasting fixed asset is capital expenditure even if it is cheap.
**Confusing a repair with an upgrade** — repairing a fixed asset is revenue expenditure; replacing or improving it with something better is capital expenditure.
**Assuming profit means cash** — a profitable business can still run out of working capital and fail. Needing day-to-day finance is normal, not a sign the business is unprofitable.

**Giving the wrong reason a business needs finance** — a start-up needs start-up capital, an established firm covering daily costs needs working capital, an expanding firm needs growth finance. Match the reason to the specific business.

**Mixing up internal and external sources** — retained profit and the sale of assets are INTERNAL; bank loans, overdrafts and share capital are EXTERNAL. Putting a source on the wrong side undermines the answer.
**Ignoring the matching principle** — funding a long-term asset with short-term finance (e.g. buying a factory on an overdraft) is a penalised error.
**Defining without applying** — bare definitions earn AO1 only; the marks climb when each term is applied to the named business in the question.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 3.1 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge and understanding, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit — but the engine rewards APPLICATION to the specific business, not a generic definition. On a short 'explain the difference' question that means the correct definitions earn the AO1 marks and the correct applied examples for the named business earn the AO2 marks. Watch how the marks below attach to accurate definitions AND to examples tied to the actual business.

sections.question: Explain the difference between capital expenditure and revenue expenditure for a named business, giving one example of each. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer (named business: 'CityCycle Ltd.', a bicycle-hire company).**
Capital expenditure is spending on fixed (non-current) assets — items a business keeps and uses for more than one year. For CityCycle Ltd., buying a fleet of new hire bicycles for £40,000 is capital expenditure, because the bikes are long-lasting assets the company will use to earn hire income over several years.

Revenue expenditure is spending on the day-to-day running of the business — recurring costs used up in the current period. For CityCycle Ltd., paying the monthly wages of its repair staff, or the electricity bill for its depot, is revenue expenditure, because these are ongoing operating costs consumed in the period they are incurred.

The key difference is lifespan: capital expenditure buys long-term assets that appear on the balance sheet, whereas revenue expenditure covers short-term running costs that appear on the income statement.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks.** The command term 'explain' with a 'named business' instruction means the engine credits correct definitions (AO1) AND correct applied examples for that business (AO2):

- **AO1 (1):** correct definition of capital expenditure — spending on fixed / non-current assets lasting more than a year.
- **AO2 (1):** a correct applied example for CityCycle — buying the fleet of hire bikes (a genuine fixed asset for THIS business).
- **AO1 (1):** correct definition of revenue expenditure — spending on day-to-day running costs used up in the period.
- **AO2 (1):** a correct applied example for CityCycle — wages of repair staff or the depot electricity bill (a genuine running cost for THIS business).

The engine rewards application, not generic recall: writing only the two definitions with no named business and no examples caps the answer at the two AO1 marks and forfeits both AO2 marks. Equally, an example that is misclassified — calling the bikes 'revenue expenditure' — loses the application mark because the lifespan test has been applied wrongly.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the correct definitions, AO2 for examples applied to the specific business in the question. So never stop at defining the terms: name a business, give a genuine fixed-asset example for capital expenditure and a genuine running-cost example for revenue expenditure, and make sure each example is classified correctly using the lifespan test. The application is where the marks the definitions alone cannot reach are won.

This topic lays the foundation for the rest of the Finance and accounts unit. The internal-versus-external overview here opens directly into 3.2, where each source of finance is compared in detail and matched to the business situation. The idea of working capital returns in cash-flow forecasting, where you will see how a profitable business can still run short of cash. And the capital-versus-revenue-expenditure distinction underpins the financial statements — capital expenditure builds the assets on the balance sheet, revenue expenditure the costs on the income statement. Master the habit built here — identify the concept, apply it to the specific business, and classify using the lifespan and purpose tests — and you have the template that earns marks across the whole finance unit.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: explain the difference between capital and revenue expenditure for a named business, applying the concepts with correct examples

items.label: Investopedia: An Overview of Business Finance
UK Gov: Set up a business

quickCheck.prompt: A restaurant spends £18,000 on a new commercial dishwasher that it expects to use for six years. How should this be classified?
options: Revenue expenditure, because £18,000 is a large amount of money
Capital expenditure, because it buys a fixed asset lasting more than one year
Revenue expenditure, because dishwashers are used every day
Working capital, because it keeps the kitchen running
quickCheck.answer: Capital expenditure, because it buys a fixed asset lasting more than one year

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is an example of revenue expenditure?
options: Buying a delivery van
Installing a new production line
Paying the monthly wages of shop staff
Purchasing a warehouse
quickCheck.answer: Paying the monthly wages of shop staff

quickCheck.prompt: A newly launched business needs money to rent premises, buy equipment and purchase its first stock before it earns any revenue. Which need for finance does this describe?
options: Working capital
Start-up capital
Growth and expansion finance
Revenue expenditure recovery

quickCheck.prompt: What is the main purpose of working capital?
options: To buy long-term fixed assets such as machinery and buildings
To fund the opening of new branches in other countries
To cover day-to-day running costs such as wages and suppliers while waiting for customers to pay
To repay the owners' original investment in the business
quickCheck.answer: To cover day-to-day running costs such as wages and suppliers while waiting for customers to pay

quickCheck.prompt: Which pair correctly identifies one internal and one external source of finance?
options: Internal: bank loan; External: retained profit
Internal: retained profit; External: bank loan
Internal: share capital; External: sale of assets
Internal: overdraft; External: retained profit
quickCheck.answer: Internal: retained profit; External: bank loan
